senses.definition: a situation where one avoids a danger just before it is too late

examples.x: It was a close call when the car swerved in front of us, but my quick reflexes prevented an accident.
The hikers had a close call with a sudden thunderstorm, but they found shelter in a cave just in time.
The company had a close call with bankruptcy, but a last-minute investor saved it from financial ruin.
Her diagnosis was a close call because the disease was in its early stages and could have become much worse if not detected.
The pilot's skillful maneuvering averted a close call when the plane's engine failed mid-flight, ensuring the passengers' safety.

senses.definition: used for referring to a situation in which something dangerous is avoided just before it is too late for it to happen

examples.x: He had a close shave when the car's brakes failed, but he managed to stop just inches from the cliff's edge.
It was a close shave for the company, narrowly avoiding bankruptcy by securing a last-minute loan.
The hiker had a close shave with a bear in the woods but was able to slowly back away unscathed.
The detective had a close shave with the criminal, narrowly avoiding being caught during the stakeout.
The successful emergency landing of the malfunctioning plane was a close shave, with passengers relieved to have landed safely.

senses.definition: to hastily leave a place, usually because of a possible danger or threat

examples.x: When the party turned rowdy and the police were on their way, we decided it was time to get out of Dodge.
After realizing the investment was a scam, he decided to get out of Dodge before losing all his money.
When the storm approached, the campers quickly packed up to get out of Dodge and avoid getting caught in the downpour.
The argument at the bar was escalating, and John thought it wise to get out of Dodge before it got violent.

senses.definition: not causing or involving any danger at all

examples.x: With the state-of-the-art security system, your valuables will be as safe as houses in our vault.
After getting the all-clear from the doctor, she felt safe as houses about her health.
The experienced pilot made the turbulent flight feel as safe as houses for the passengers.
Investing in government bonds is considered safe as houses because they are backed by the full faith and credit of the government.
In this small, tight-knit community, leaving your door unlocked at night is as safe as houses; everyone knows and trusts each other.

word_list.pronunciation: sˈeɪv wˈʌnz skˈɪn nˈɛk hˈaɪd bˈeɪkən

senses.definition: to help a person get out of a situation that involves danger or difficulty

examples.x: But a truck driver saved her bacon.
Thanks for bringing me some extra cash—you really saved my skin, there!
In the face of the oncoming tornado, we sought shelter in the basement to save our skins.
The quick-thinking lifeguard rescued the drowning swimmer and saved his neck.
He was willing to cooperate with the authorities to save his bacon and avoid prosecution.

senses.definition: as carefully as possible in order to avoid any risk

examples.x: You probably won't need to apply for planning permission to build a shed in your garden, but to be on the safe side, check with your local planning department.
We budgeted an extra hour for the journey, just to be on the safe side in case of traffic.
The doctor prescribed a lower dosage of the medication to be on the safe side, considering potential side effects.
Before leaving for the trip, he packed an emergency kit with supplies on the safe side, in case of unforeseen situations.
When planning the event, the organizers scheduled an additional hour for setup and testing to be on the safe side and avoid any delays.

word_list.pronunciation: mˌeɪk ɔːɹ bˈiːt ɐ hˈeɪsti ɔːɹ ɹɪtɹˈiːt

senses.definition: to escape or withdraw from a situation that is dangerous or unpleasant

examples.x: The team then beats a retreat from the mall, hoping no one noticed them.
As the weather worsened, the hikers decided to make a retreat to the safety of their camp.
Realizing the project was not going as planned, the team leader suggested making a retreat to reassess their approach.

senses.definition: to be out of a situation that is full of danger, trouble, or hardship

examples.x: With the successful completion of the major project milestones, we can confidently say that we are out of the woods in terms of meeting our deadline.
It took a lot of hard work, but we are out of the woods with our legal troubles behind us.
The storm was intense, but now that it has passed, we can say we're out of the woods and the worst is over.

senses.definition: to unexpectedly provide someone with help when they face a danger or difficulty

examples.x: Just when he was about to start yelling at me, the fire alarm went off and saved me by the bell.

senses.definition: no longer facing a difficulty, danger, or punishment

examples.x: Thanks to the extension granted by the teacher, I'm off the hook for submitting the assignment today.
After a thorough investigation, the detective realized the suspect was innocent and let him off the hook.
His apology and sincere regret got him off the hook with his angry boss.
After receiving a full refund and an apology, the customer felt that the company had taken responsibility and let them off the hook for the inconvenience.

senses.definition: used when a person or thing is being looked after by a trustworthy and capable individual

examples.x: Leaving our children with Grandma and Grandpa means they are in safe hands.
With the experienced team of doctors at this hospital, I know I'm in good hands for my surgery.
When we handed over the project to the skilled project manager, we knew it was in safe hands.
Knowing that the security company is guarding our premises, we feel confident that our property is in good hands.
The CEO assured the shareholders that the company was in safe hands under the new leadership.

senses.definition: to narrowly avoid a dangerous or undesirable situation

examples.x: I was supposed to be on that flight, but I canceled at the last minute, so I really dodged a bullet when I heard about the crash.
Getting out of the toxic relationship was tough, but I know I dodged a bullet by not marrying that person.
He almost invested all his savings in that company just before it went bankrupt; he really dodged a bullet there.
After a routine health check, the doctor told her that her early diagnosis helped her dodge a bullet by catching the disease before it progressed.
The detective managed to dodge a bullet when he narrowly avoided a trap set by the criminals during the investigation.

senses.definition: used to say that everything is safe and someone can proceed to do something
to survive a danger or difficulty and be able to tell others about it

examples.x: Despite the harrowing plane crash, all the passengers survived and lived to tell the tale.
After a vicious encounter with a wild animal, the hiker was relieved to live to tell the tale.
The firefighter narrowly escaped a collapsing building and lived to tell the tale of the rescue mission.
The sailor faced a severe storm at sea but managed to live to tell the tale of his survival.
The young cancer survivor has a compelling story to share about her journey to live to tell the tale of recovery and resilience.

senses.definition: in a place or position that is safe from danger or damage

examples.x: After the wildfire broke out, the residents were evacuated to a shelter to keep them out of harm's way.
The government provided emergency housing for the victims of the flood, ensuring they were out of harm's way.
The troops were ordered to retreat to a secure location to get them out of harm's way during the battle.
The building was constructed with earthquake-resistant materials to keep the occupants out of harm's way in case of a seismic event.
The wildlife sanctuary was established to provide a safe environment where endangered species could be out of harm's way and protected from poachers.

word_list.pronunciation: kˈiːp ɔːɹ hˈoʊld ˌɛsbˈiː slˈæʃ ˌɛstˌiːˈeɪtʃ æt bˈeɪ

senses.definition: to maintain control or prevent someone or something from advancing, causing harm, or becoming a threat

examples.x: The guard dog kept intruders at bay during the night.
The negotiator's skillful communication kept the hostile parties at bay, preventing a conflict.
